common_name: Ruby Loropetalum (Compact Deep-Purple Foliage Gulf South Shrub)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 5.5–6.5) soil in full sun to partial shade; Ruby maintains the most intense burgundy-purple foliage color in full sun — partial shade causes the foliage to turn more greenish-purple in color.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Ruby is a compact Loropetalum chinense (Chinese fringe flower) cultivar producing deep, rich burgundy-purple to reddish-purple evergreen foliage throughout the year, punctuated by prolific flushes of bright magenta-pink, fringe-petaled flowers that create a vivid burgundy-and-magenta combination in spring (March–April) and sporadic rebloom through fall. At 4–6 feet with a similar spread, Ruby is one of the most manageable Loropetalum cultivars for typical residential borders and foundation plantings — the compact, rounded habit is more landscape-appropriate than the large-growing species which can reach 10–15 feet. The deep burgundy foliage holds its rich purple color well through the Gulf South summer without the greening that affects some purple-leaf shrubs in high heat. Loropetalum tolerates considerable drought once established and handles the acidic, often clay soils of the Gulf South coast without significant amendment. The spring bloom is one of the showiest shrub floral displays of the Gulf South spring garden — the combination of the bright magenta flowers against the dark burgundy foliage is visually stunning. Minimal maintenance required: prune immediately after spring bloom to maintain size and shape; avoid late-season pruning that removes flower buds developing for the following spring.

common_name: Purple Pixie Loropetalum (Dwarf Weeping Groundcover Fringe Flower)

planting.note: Plant container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Purple Pixie's strongly weeping habit makes it exceptional for trailing over retaining walls, spilling out of raised planters, and massing as a groundcover on slopes — uses that exploit rather than fight its natural growth form.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; Purple Pixie is a dwarf, strongly weeping Loropetalum cultivar with a radically different growth habit from standard upright loropetalum — growing very low (12–24 inches tall) but spreading wide (3–5 feet) with strongly pendulous, cascading branches that create an elegant, trailing, mounded groundcover form unlike any other commonly available ornamental shrub in the Gulf South. The weeping habit makes Purple Pixie exceptional for uses that standard upright shrubs cannot fill: draping over retaining walls and raised planter edges to create a cascading curtain of burgundy foliage, massing as a groundcover on slopes and banks that are difficult to maintain with upright plants, and providing low-growing, weed-suppressing cover in landscape beds where taller shrubs would block views. The same deep burgundy-purple foliage and bright magenta-pink spring flowers as standard loropetalum cultivars, produced on cascading branches that sweep naturally downward. Extremely drought-tolerant once established, tolerating heat, humidity, and the clay soils of the Gulf South. The very compact, spreading growth means minimal pruning is required to maintain the natural form — periodic tip-pruning to encourage density and remove any particularly vigorous upright shoots maintains the groundcover character.

common_name: Gold Rush Zucchini (AAS-Winning Yellow-Skinned Summer Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; in zone 9, sow spring crops in February–March and fall crops in August–September for the best production before summer heat or fall cold limits the season.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Gold Rush is an All-America Selections-winning yellow zucchini hybrid — producing the classic straight, cylindrical zucchini form (6–8 inches at optimal harvest) in brilliant golden-yellow skin rather than the standard dark-green of typical zucchini. The golden-yellow skin is visually striking and creates attractive contrast with standard green zucchini in market displays, mixed vegetable dishes, and grilled presentations — the same tender, mild, slightly sweet flesh as green zucchini in a color that makes it immediately distinguishable. Gold Rush is a compact bush-type plant reaching 18–24 inches with a moderate 3–4 foot spread — appropriate for typical garden spacing without the sprawling habit of some summer squash. The AAS recognition reflects exceptional performance across multiple growing regions and conditions. In zone 9, Gold Rush is best grown as a spring crop (February–March planting for April–May harvest) or fall crop (August planting for September–October harvest) — summer heat causes powdery mildew and reduced production. Harvest at 6–8 inches for best flavor and tenderness; oversized fruits become seedy and tough. Squash vine borers are the primary pest challenge in zone 9; monitoring and early action against eggs is essential for spring production.

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; in zone 9, sow spring cucumbers in February–March and fall cucumbers in late July–August for the best production in the shoulder seasons before summer heat or fall cold limits the crop.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Marketmore 80 is a Cornell University slicing cucumber introduction that has become one of the most widely grown home garden cucumbers in America — producing straight, cylindrical, 8–9 inch, deep-green cucumbers with excellent fresh-eating flavor, thin skin, and small seed cavities. The significant disease resistance package — scab, cucumber mosaic virus (CMV), and angular leaf spot resistance — makes Marketmore 80 substantially more reliable in Gulf South gardens where summer humidity drives disease pressure. The 'Marketmore' series from Cornell represents a succession of improvements beginning with the original Marketmore in the 1960s; Marketmore 80 was selected for improved scab resistance and CMV tolerance critical for humid-climate production. The non-bitter fruits have classic cucumber flavor and good crispness. Plants are indeterminate vines that perform best on a trellis or fence where air circulation reduces disease and fruits hang straight. In zone 9, cucumbers are most productive in the spring season (March–May) and fall season (August–October) — summer heat causes reduced fruiting and increased disease. Succession sowing every 2–3 weeks through spring extends the harvest window. Harvest fruits at 6–9 inches for best flavor; over-mature cucumbers become bitter and seeds harden.

common_name: Pickle Bush Cucumber (Compact Bush-Habit Pickling Cucumber for Small Spaces)

planting.note: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pickle Bush's compact, bush-habit vines (18–24 inch spread) make it excellent for raised beds and container growing — no trellis required and the plants are self-supporting.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Pickle Bush is a bush-type pickling cucumber producing compact, self-supporting vines 18–24 inches across rather than the sprawling 4–6 foot vines of standard pickling cucumbers — making it the preferred choice for raised beds, containers, and small gardens where trellises are impractical and space is limited. The short (3–4 inch), blocky, warted, pickling-type cucumbers mature very early (48–55 days) in prolific clusters that concentrate harvest in a short window ideal for batch processing and canning. Pickling cucumbers are specifically bred for thin, tender skin that absorbs brine quickly, mild-to-bland flavor that allows pickle seasonings to dominate, and firm flesh that maintains crunchiness through the pickling process — qualities that distinguish them from slicing cucumbers in important culinary ways. In zone 9, the very early maturity (48–55 days) of Pickle Bush is a practical advantage — it completes its production cycle before summer heat drives cucumbers into bitter, poor-quality fruit stage. The bush habit allows high-density planting in raised beds (18 inches apart) compared to the 3–4 foot spacing required for trellised vining types. AAS Honorable Mention recognized Pickle Bush's outstanding performance in the small-space home garden category.
